US climber scales Taiwan's tallest building with no ropes
data.description:
STORY: ::U.S. climber Alex Honnold scales the 1,667-foot-tall Taipei 101 skyscraper with no safety gear

::Taipei, Taiwan

::January 25, 2026

::Alex Honnold, U.S. climber

"I first saw Taipei 101 over ten years ago and was like, wow, that's incredible for climbing, that would be amazing, but at the time didn't get the opportunity to actually climb it. // I thought about climbing it without permission, you know, don't tell anybody, you know, but I was kind of like, oh, I know I can do it and it would be amazing, like maybe I should. But then out of respect for the building and respect for all the the people on the team who had allowed me access to look at it, I was like, well, obviously I'm not going to poach this. Like I'm going to, you know, respect the people and just see if it ever comes together. And so then for the project to come together more than a decade later, oh my god, it's so great, what an opportunity."

"Yeah, it was like, what a view, it's incredible. What a beautiful day. It was very windy. So I was like, don't fall off the spire. I was like trying to balance nicely, but I was like, what an incredible position and, I mean, what a beautiful way to see Taipei."

At 1,667 feet (508 meters), the Taipei 101 is Taiwan's tallest building and a major tourist attraction. It was the tallest in the world from 2004 to 2010, a crown currently held by the Burj Khalifa in Dubai. 

The climb, with no safety equipment, took place with the full support and permission of Taipei 101 and the city government. It was organised and broadcast live by Netflix

French climber Alain Robert, dubbed "Spiderman" for his ropeless ascents of some of the world's highest skyscrapers, climbed the Taipei 101 in 2004, though he did so with a safety rope, in a time of four hours.

data.title: Canada respects its commitments under USMCA, PM Mark Carney says
Location with Guarantees – Billions Flow into Germany
data.description:
DÜSSELDORF (dpa-AFX) – Ever-present tariff threats and escalating trade conflicts: In this uncertain environment, Germany is once again attracting more investment. For the first time in a long while, foreign companies invested more money in Germany in 2025 than German companies invested abroad, according to a recent analysis. “In an increasingly uncertain world, predictability matters more than ever,” says Jürgen Matthes, trade expert at the German Economic Institute (IW).

Recent headlines offer a glimpse into the situation: German automaker Volkswagen is holding back on its plans for an Audi plant in the United States, citing tariffs as a financial obstacle. Meanwhile, U.S. President Donald Trump continues to use tariff threats as a political lever, warning Canada of 100 percent tariffs to prevent closer economic ties between the neighboring country and China.

Germany Scores with Legal Certainty

The erratic policies of the U.S. president harm not only partners but also the American economy itself, Matthes emphasizes. “Legal certainty and predictability have become key advantages for Germany in global competition.” Germany also remains highly attractive as a research hub, he adds.

Experts at the employer-affiliated institute estimate that foreign direct investment in Germany more than doubled from 43 billion euros in 2024 to 96 billion euros last year. In contrast, German companies invested a below-average amount abroad: 86 billion euros. These estimates are based on figures from the German Bundesbank for the first eleven months of 2025.

Economists Call Tariffs an “Own Goal”

Whether less was invested in the U.S. at the same time remains unclear, as complete data for 2025 are not yet available. Studies indicate, however, that tariffs are harming the U.S. economy—American consumers ultimately bear the costs through higher prices. The Kiel Institute for the World Economy (IfW) recently described Trump's approach as an “own goal.”

Trump's tariff policy is also hurting the German economy, which is struggling to sell products. The Ifo Institute estimates that Trump tariffs will reduce German economic growth by 0.3 percentage points in 2025 and forecasts a 0.6 percentage point impact for 2026. The automotive sector is particularly hard hit. As a result, Trump is also weakening potential investors, such as Volkswagen.

VW CEO: Audi Plant Not Feasible Due to Tariffs

This burdened the VW Group with 2.1 billion euros in the first nine months of 2025, according to CEO Oliver Blume in an interview with the “Handelsblatt.” Volkswagen can only proceed with the planned Audi plant in the U.S. if Trump reduces the tariff burden. “With tariffs remaining at current levels, a major additional investment is not financially feasible,” Blume stated. He called for cost relief and reliable framework conditions.

Meanwhile, U.S. President Donald Trump is threatening Canada. Should the neighboring country strike a deal with China, all Canadian goods imported into the U.S. would immediately face a 100 percent tariff, the Republican wrote on his Truth Social platform. If Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney thinks he can turn Canada into a “transshipment hub” for China to get Chinese goods into the U.S., he is gravely mistaken, Trump warned.

Recently, Carney had traveled to Beijing and subsequently announced plans for greater cooperation with China./bf/DP/jha

India to slash tariffs on cars to 40% in trade deal with EU, sources say
data.description:
NEW DELHI, Jan 25 (Reuters) - India plans to slash tariffs on cars imported from the European Union to 40% from as high as 110%, sources said, in the biggest opening yet of the country's vast market as the two sides close in on a free trade pact that could come as early as Tuesday.

Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government has agreed to immediately reduce the tax on a limited number of cars from the 27-nation bloc with an import price of more than 15,000 euros ($17,739), two sources briefed on the talks told Reuters.

This will be further lowered to 10% over time, they added, easing access to the Indian market for European automakers such as Volkswagen, Mercedes-Benz and BMW.

The sources declined to be identified as the talks are confidential and could be subject to last-minute changes. India's commerce ministry and the European Commission declined to comment.

PACT ALREADY DUBBED 'MOTHER OF ALL DEALS'

India and the EU are expected to announce on Tuesday the conclusion of protracted negotiations for the free trade pact, after which the two sides will finalise the details and ratify what is being called "the mother of all deals.

The pact could expand bilateral trade and lift Indian exports of goods such as textiles and jewellery, which have been hit by 50% U.S. tariffs since late August.

India is the world's third-largest car market by sales after the U.S. and China, but its domestic auto industry has been one of the most protected. New Delhi currently levies tariffs of 70% and 110% on imported cars, a level often criticised by executives, including Tesla chief Elon Musk.

New Delhi has proposed slashing import duties to 40% immediately for about 200,000 combustion-engine cars a year, one of the sources said, its most aggressive move yet to open up the sector. This quota could be subject to last-minute changes, the source added.

Battery electric vehicles will be excluded from import duty reductions for the first five years to protect investments by domestic players like Mahindra & Mahindra and Tata Motors in the nascent sector, the two sources said. After five years EVs will follow similar duty cuts.

MARKET CURRENTLY DOMINATED BY SUZUKI AND LOCAL MAKERS

Lower import taxes will be a boost for European automakers such as Volkswagen, Renault and Stellantis, as well as luxury players Mercedes-Benz and BMW which locally manufacture cars in India but have struggled to grow beyond a point in part due to high tariffs.

Lower taxes will allow carmakers to sell imported vehicles for a cheaper price and test the market with a broader portfolio before committing to manufacturing more cars locally, said one of the two sources.

European carmakers currently hold a less than 4% share of India's 4.4-million units a year car market, which is dominated by Japan's Suzuki Motor as well as homegrown brands Mahindra and Tata that together hold two-thirds.

With the Indian market expected to grow to 6 million units a year by 2030, some companies are already lining up new investment.

Renault is making a comeback in India with a new strategy as it seeks growth outside Europe, where Chinese carmakers are making strong inroads, and Volkswagen Group is finalising its next leg of investment in India through its Skoda brand.

(Reporting by Aditi Shah and Phil Blenkinsop; Additional reporting by Philip Blenkinsop and Lili Bayer in Brussels, with Shivangi Acharya in New Delhi; Editing by David Holmes)

By Aditi Shah

Deutsche Bank Bets on AI and Further Trims Branch Network
data.description:
FRANKFURT (dpa-AFX) - More digitalization, fewer branches: Deutsche Bank is continuing to overhaul its retail banking business. "By the end of 2026, around 100 more locations will be closed as part of existing programs, following reductions in recent years," said Claudio de Sanctis, Head of Retail Banking, to the news agencies dpa and dpa-AFX in Frankfurt. "This puts us on track toward a branch network that is appropriate for us." At the end of last year, Deutsche Bank and Postbank together operated just over 750 branches in Germany, according to company figures.

"I am firmly convinced that we still need branches," de Sanctis emphasized. At the same time, work in the branches must continue to evolve. "Currently, our employees in the branches spend too much of their working time on administrative tasks. But they should have more time for customer consultations." The challenge, he said, is to shift administrative tasks to digital channels and free up more time for clients.

AI in Customer Service

To attract more customers to digital offerings such as the bank's new app, Deutsche Bank is currently developing a digital agent, an AI-powered assistant, for customer service. "In the second half of the year, we will offer customers a digital assistant, a kind of butler, who will initially explain how to use the app. Customers can ask it any questions, and it will show them step by step how things work."

The bank has increased app usage by nearly 20 percent over the past 18 months. "However, we want to further drastically increase the number of active app users, because Deutsche Bank and Postbank together have 19 million retail customers in Germany. Getting as many customers as possible to use the app is one of the main goals for our activities this year," said the head of retail banking./ben/stw/zb

data.title: VW: Audi Plant in the US Not Financially Viable Due to Tariffs
data.description:
WOLFSBURG (dpa-AFX) - Volkswagen can only proceed with the proposed Audi plant in the United States if President Donald Trump reduces the burden of tariffs. This was made clear in an interview with the "Handelsblatt" by Oliver Blume, the head of the Volkswagen Group, to which Audi belongs. "With the current level of tariffs unchanged, a major additional investment is not financially viable," Blume responded to a related question. "What we need are short-term cost relief measures and long-term reliable framework conditions."

Audi has been considering building a plant in the US since 2023. At the time, new subsidies in the United States were cited as a reason, making the construction of a plant economically attractive. However, the Trump administration is now putting pressure on European carmakers with tariffs. According to Blume, this weighed on the VW Group by 2.1 billion euros in the first nine months of 2025.

Nevertheless, the CEO spoke of a "forward strategy" for the US business and significant growth opportunities. However, Blume described the previously stated goal of a ten percent market share in the US as an old target. Today, Volkswagen must move forward step by step.

There have been some discussions with the government in Washington regarding the construction of an Audi plant, including talks with President Trump and his Secretary of Commerce, Howard Lutnick – but so far, without the necessary results. "Those who invest, create jobs, and generate added value must also benefit on the cost side," Blume demanded. "We remain open to solutions that benefit both sides."/bf/DP/zb
